Okay, okay, I think that's enough. The point of this exercise was to show you how uncomfortable saying yes could be. Salespeople often have a scripted flow chart that is designed to cut off your escape routes and funnel you down a path with no choice but to keep saying yes. They want you to say yes at all costs and avoid letting you say no like the plague. But even if you did say no, their script cuts you off and brings you back in. I promise that this is a very poor negotiation tactic. Who likes to be trapped? This approach is probably the single biggest cause of people who just stop returning your calls or talking to you. It's a corrosive that slowly destroys trust and relationships. No one trusts someone who traps them. The problem is that in our society the word no has tons of negative connotations. We are taught to fear hearing the word because it is the ultimate negative word in existence. However, what did you notice about saying the word yes during our exercise? It was both meaningless and uncomfortable. It was kind of fit. It was just so you could buy enough time to find out if there was any information you wanted before you made good your escape. You didn't mean it. The word yes oftentimes hides deeper objections. And if you push too hard you don't necessarily get any closer to a win. Your counterpart just gets frustrated and angry because they don't want to say yes. They just feel like they have to just like you did when the salesman was asking you about food. For a great negotiator the word no is solid gold. This simple two-letter word provides a great opportunity for you and the other party to clarify what you really want by eliminating what you don't want. The word no is a safe choice. By letting someone say it it makes them feel safe and in control. They say kids are good at negotiating with their parents because they won't take no for an answer. The truth is that kids have learned how much more people, parents in this case, are willing to listen once they've said no. At some point you'll have to come to terms with the word no. In this course we will cover the psychological dynamic behind no. You'll begin to love it. Yes, and maybe, are often worthless. No always alters the conversation. You learn how to make this universal constant work in your favor.
